download_details.title: The Economics of SMRs: Why Simplicity and Scalability Matter

findings: Large nuclear reactors were intentionally scaled up to exploit economies of scale; utilities ordered plants exceeding 1,000 MW before any reactor larger than 250 MW was even operational.

SMR staff operating costs per unit output can be as much as 190% higher than the fixed operating cost of fewer large reactors, and a German BASE study estimates an average of 3,000 SMRs must be produced before SMR production becomes cost-worthwhile.

The first US SMR deployment—NuScale's Carbon Free Power Project—was cancelled in November 2023 after its estimated generation cost surged from $58/MWh in 2021 to $89/MWh in 2023, raising broader concerns about SMR commercial viability.
Australian CSIRO estimated in 2024 that electricity from an SMR built from 2023 would cost roughly 2.5× that of a traditional large nuclear plant, declining to about 1.6× by 2030.
An October 2023 academic study of 19 SMR designs found none were profitable or economically competitive, with median LCOEs for closer-to-market PWR SMRs ranging from $218/MWh to $614/MWh (2020 USD).
